showRect.caption: Climate Connections How Earth's Systems Tell One Story This tour explores how climate change shows up across our planet — in temperature, ice, oceans, ecosystems, and the carbon cycle. Each dataset reveals a piece of the puzzle.
Let's start with the big picture — how has Earth's temperature changed over the past 170 years?

This map shows temperature anomalies — how much warmer or cooler each year is compared to the 20th century average.

<color=green>Blue means cooler than average. Red and orange mean warmer.</color>

Watch how the colors shift from mostly blue to mostly red as the animation plays forward through time.

Notice how the Arctic warms much faster than the rest of the planet. This is called Arctic amplification — as ice melts, the darker ocean absorbs more heat, causing even more warming.\n\nWhere else do you see intense warming? Why might land warm faster than oceans?
If the Arctic is warming so fast, what's happening to the ice? Let's look at decades of satellite data on sea ice.

The bright white areas show the oldest, thickest sea ice — some of it over 9 years old. The darker blues show younger, thinner ice that formed recently. Watch what happens over the decades: the old ice nearly vanishes. What happens to Arctic wildlife — polar bears, seals, walruses — when the ice they depend on disappears?
Melting ice and warming water both cause sea levels to rise. Where are the most vulnerable places on Earth?

Bangladesh is one of the most vulnerable nations. Over 160 million people live in this low-lying delta. Even a small rise in sea level can displace millions and contaminate fresh water supplies with salt water.

South Florida sits just a few feet above sea level. Miami already experiences regular tidal flooding — streets that flood on sunny days simply because the tide is high.\n\nWhat challenges does rising water create for coastal cities?
The ocean absorbs about 30% of the CO₂ we release. That sounds helpful — but it comes at a cost.

This shows the saturation state of aragonite — a mineral that corals, shellfish, and many marine organisms need to build their shells and skeletons. As CO₂ dissolves in seawater, it forms carbonic acid, lowering the pH and making it harder for these creatures to survive. We're looking at Australia's Great Barrier Reef — the largest living structure on Earth. Why are coral reefs called 'the rainforests of the sea'?
Now let's look at the source of the problem — carbon dioxide itself. Watch how it moves through the atmosphere.

This is CarbonTracker — a model that traces CO₂ from its sources (fossil fuels, fires) through the atmosphere. Notice the seasonal pulse: every spring and summer, forests in the Northern Hemisphere absorb massive amounts of CO₂, and the concentrations drop. In fall and winter, they release it back. Can you see the 'breathing' of the planet? Watch the Northern Hemisphere cycle between high and low concentrations.
Those forests that absorb CO₂ — let's see them from space. Earth's vegetation is one of our greatest allies against climate change.

The Amazon rainforest — the largest tropical rainforest on Earth. It produces about 6% of the world's oxygen and stores enormous amounts of carbon.\n\nWatch the seasonal cycle: green intensifies in the wet season, fades slightly in the dry season. This is Earth breathing.

The boreal forests of Russia, Canada, and Scandinavia cover more area than the Amazon. They're a massive carbon sink — but they're also warming faster than almost anywhere else.\n\nAs permafrost thaws and forests shift northward, what might happen to the carbon stored in these soils?

Climate Connections

Every dataset in this tour tells part of the same story:

• Rising temperatures amplify in the Arctic
• Sea ice that took decades to form disappears in years
• Oceans rise and acidify as they absorb heat and CO₂
• The carbon cycle pulses with the seasons — but the baseline keeps climbing
• Forests are our allies, but they're under pressure too

<color=green>These systems are all connected. Changes in one ripple through the others. Understanding these connections is the first step toward solutions.</color>
Thanks for exploring Climate Connections!\n\nKeep exploring — browse more datasets, ask Orbit questions, or run this tour again anytime.
